concept_name: The Bar Magnet as a Magnetic Dipole — closed-loop field, no monopole, m, 1/r³
source_book: NCERT Class 12 Physics Part 1, Chapter 5 (Magnetism and Matter) §5.2 (The Bar Magnet) — table-of-contents reference only; teaching authored from first principles. NCERT consulted for the Indian real-world anchor (the school-lab bar magnet + iron-filings demo and the magnetic compass). The atomic concept taught here is what a bar magnet IS: a MAGNETIC DIPOLE. Its magnetic field lines are continuous CLOSED loops — they emerge from the N pole, curve around outside to the S pole, and continue INSIDE the magnet from S back to N, so a line never starts or ends (unlike electric field lines, which begin on + charge and end on − charge). A direct consequence is that there is NO isolated magnetic pole (no magnetic monopole): break a bar magnet and you get two complete smaller magnets, each with its own N and S — new poles appear at the cut. The bar magnet carries a magnetic moment m that points from S to N, and it is the EQUIVALENT of a solenoid of moment m = NIA, producing the same far field. That far field is the dipole field, falling as 1/r³: at the same distance r the AXIAL field (along the magnet's axis) is exactly TWICE the EQUATORIAL field (perpendicular to it) — B_axial = (μ₀/4π)(2m/r³), B_equatorial = (μ₀/4π)(m/r³). This is the exact electrostatic ANALOG of an electric dipole (m ↔ p, μ₀/4π ↔ 1/4πε₀). The deepest student confusions targeted: (M1) field lines start at N and end at S like charges; (M2) you can isolate a north pole by cutting the magnet; (M3) magnetism is unrelated to electric current; (M4) the axial and equatorial fields are equal; (M5) the dipole field falls as 1/r² like a point charge. CUT-LINE: this concept teaches the bar magnet's IDENTITY and FIELD only — it does NOT cover the torque/oscillation/energy of a bar magnet in an external uniform field (τ = m × B, U = −m·B), which is the sibling concept bar_magnet_in_uniform_field, nor Gauss's law for magnetism (∮B·dA = 0), which is gauss_law_magnetism. Conceptual-only ship (no mode_overrides). EPIC-L-first (no epic_c_branches; the five misconceptions are confronted inside EPIC-L via Rule 16a misconception_watch + predict→reveal).

real_world_anchor.primary: Picture the red-and-blue bar magnet from your school physics lab, the one you slid under a sheet of paper sprinkled with iron filings — the filings snapped into curved lines arcing from one end to the other. Those curves are exactly the field you will see here, and this concept finally explains why they form closed loops and why you can never shake out a single magnetic pole.
real_world_anchor.secondary: Picture the small magnetic compass needle used in your geography or physics class. That needle is itself a tiny bar magnet — a magnetic dipole — and it lines up tangent to the very field lines drawn here. Its red end is the magnet's N, pointing along the field.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: Picture snapping a fridge magnet or a magnetic strip in half. You might expect one piece to keep the north and the other the south — but each broken piece is instantly a complete magnet again, with a fresh N and S at the break. That stubborn fact is the no-monopole rule.

m.name: Magnetic moment of the bar magnet

formulas.field_lines_closed: Magnetic field lines form continuous CLOSED loops: out of N, around outside to S, and through the magnet from S back to N — never starting or ending.
formulas.no_monopole: No isolated magnetic pole exists: cut a bar magnet and each piece is a complete dipole, with a new N and S appearing at the cut.
formulas.moment: m = N I A — the bar magnet is equivalent to a solenoid of N turns, current I, area A; m points from S to N.
formulas.axial_field: B_axial = (mu0 / 4pi) * (2 m / r^3) — on the axis, distance r >> magnet length.
formulas.equatorial_field: B_equatorial = (mu0 / 4pi) * (m / r^3) — perpendicular to the axis, same r.
formulas.axial_twice_equatorial: B_axial = 2 * B_equatorial at the same distance r; both fall as 1 / r^3 (a dipole field, NOT 1/r^2).
formulas.electrostatic_analog: Exact analog of an electric dipole: m <-> p, mu0/4pi <-> 1/(4pi*eps0); the field patterns are identical.

constraints: Magnetic field lines are continuous and closed - they have no start or end point, unlike electric field lines which begin on positive charge and end on negative charge.
There is no magnetic monopole: breaking a magnet always yields two dipoles, never an isolated N or S.
The magnetic moment m points from the S pole to the N pole, with magnitude m = N I A (the equivalent solenoid).
The dipole far field falls as 1/r^3 (not 1/r^2): on the axis B = (mu0/4pi)(2m/r^3), on the equatorial line B = (mu0/4pi)(m/r^3).
At equal distance r the axial field is exactly TWICE the equatorial field, and the two point in opposite senses relative to m (axial along m, equatorial opposite to m).
The bar magnet is the exact magnetic analog of an electric dipole (m plays the role of p, mu0/4pi plays the role of 1/4pi*eps0).

aha_moment.statement: Break a bar magnet and you never get a lone pole — only two dipoles.
aha_moment.visual_confirmation: STATE_3 splits the magnet at its centre; brand-new N and S poles light up at the cut, and the field rearranges into two complete dipoles — repeating to show it happens no matter where you break it.

STATE_1.title: A bar magnet and its field — a compass lines up tangent

scene_composition.text: A bar magnet (N red, S blue) and the field around it

Green lines = magnetic field B.
Amber dots drift along them,
showing the field direction —
out of N, into S.

Like iron filings snapping into
curves under your lab magnet.
A compass needle settles
tangent to the field.
Question: what kind of object is a bar magnet, really — and what is this field?

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is a bar magnet — north pole red, south pole blue — with its magnetic field B drawn in green around it.
tts_sentences.text_hi: यहाँ एक bar magnet है — north pole लाल, south pole नीला — इसके चारों ओर magnetic field B हरे रंग में।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఇక్కడ ఒక bar magnet ఉంది — north pole ఎరుపు, south pole నీలం — దాని చుట్టూ magnetic field B ఆకుపచ్చ రంగులో.

tts_sentences.text_en: The amber dots drift along the field lines, showing the field direction — out of the north pole, around, into the south.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Amber dots field lines के साथ बहते हैं, field की दिशा दिखाते हैं — north pole से बाहर, घूमकर, south में।
tts_sentences.text_te: Amber dots field lines వెంట కదులుతాయి, field దిశను చూపుతాయి — north pole నుండి బయటకు, చుట్టూ, south లోకి.

tts_sentences.text_en: A compass needle settles tangent to the field. So what kind of object is a bar magnet, really?
tts_sentences.text_hi: एक compass needle field के tangent settle होती है। तो bar magnet असल में किस तरह की वस्तु है?
tts_sentences.text_te: ఒక compass needle field కి tangent గా settle అవుతుంది. మరి bar magnet నిజంగా ఎలాంటి వస్తువు?

misconception_watch.belief: A magnet's field is just a glow around it, with no definite direction.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: Amber dots drift consistently out of the north pole and into the south along each line, and a compass needle settles tangent to the field.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: The magnetic field has a definite direction at every point — out of N, into S outside the magnet — and a compass lines up tangent to it.

STATE_2.title: The field lines are CLOSED loops — unlike electric field lines

scene_composition.text: Follow ONE field line all the way around

It leaves the N pole and
curves to the S pole. Where
does it go from there —
does it just stop at S?

No — it continues INSIDE the
magnet, from S back to N.
The line closes on itself: a
continuous loop, no end.
Magnetic field lines are CLOSED loops — electric field lines start on + and end on −

tts_sentences.text_en: Follow one field line. It leaves the north pole and curves around the outside toward the south pole.
tts_sentences.text_hi: एक field line follow कीजिए। यह north pole से निकलती है और बाहर से घूमकर south pole की ओर जाती है।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఒక field line ను follow చేయండి. అది north pole నుండి బయలుదేరి, బయట నుండి south pole వైపు వంగుతుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: Where does it go from there? Does the line simply stop at the south pole?
tts_sentences.text_hi: वहाँ से यह कहाँ जाती है? क्या line बस south pole पर रुक जाती है?
tts_sentences.text_te: అక్కడ నుండి అది ఎక్కడికి వెళుతుంది? Line కేవలం south pole వద్ద ఆగిపోతుందా?

tts_sentences.text_en: No — it continues right through the magnet, from south back to north. The line closes on itself.
tts_sentences.text_hi: नहीं — यह magnet के अंदर से होकर, south से वापस north जाती है। Line अपने आप पर बंद हो जाती है।
tts_sentences.text_te: కాదు — అది magnet లోపలి నుండి, south నుండి తిరిగి north కి వెళుతుంది. Line తనపై తాను మూసుకుంటుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: Magnetic field lines are continuous closed loops — unlike electric field lines, which start on positive charge and end on negative.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Magnetic field lines continuous closed loops हैं — electric field lines के विपरीत, जो positive charge पर शुरू और negative पर खत्म होती हैं।
tts_sentences.text_te: Magnetic field lines continuous closed loops — electric field lines కి భిన్నంగా, అవి positive charge వద్ద మొదలై negative వద్ద ముగుస్తాయి.

misconception_watch.belief: Magnetic field lines start at the north pole and end at the south pole, just like electric field lines on charges.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: A bright tracer dot rides one field line out of N, around to S, and then continues straight through the magnet from S back to N — closing the loop.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: Magnetic field lines never start or end; they are continuous closed loops that pass through the magnet from S to N inside.

STATE_3.title: No magnetic monopole — break it and get two dipoles

scene_composition.text: Cut the bar magnet exactly in half

Predict: do you get one piece
that is pure north and another
that is pure south — an
isolated single pole?

No — each half is a complete
magnet. Brand-new N and S
poles appear at the cut.
You can never isolate a pole.
No magnetic monopole: every cut makes two dipoles — keep cutting, always two poles

tts_sentences.text_en: Now cut the bar magnet exactly in half at its middle.
tts_sentences.text_hi: अब bar magnet को इसके बीच में ठीक आधा काटिए।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఇప్పుడు bar magnet ను దాని మధ్యలో సరిగ్గా సగానికి కట్ చేయండి.

tts_sentences.text_en: Predict: do you get one piece that is pure north and another that is pure south — a single isolated pole?
tts_sentences.text_hi: Predict कीजिए: क्या आपको एक टुकड़ा pure north और दूसरा pure south मिलता है — एक single isolated pole?
tts_sentences.text_te: Predict చేయండి: మీకు ఒక ముక్క pure north, మరొకటి pure south వస్తుందా — ఒక single isolated pole?

tts_sentences.text_en: No — each half is instantly a complete magnet. Brand-new north and south poles appear at the cut.
tts_sentences.text_hi: नहीं — हर आधा टुकड़ा तुरंत एक complete magnet है। Cut पर बिल्कुल नए north और south poles आ जाते हैं।
tts_sentences.text_te: కాదు — ప్రతి సగం వెంటనే ఒక complete magnet అవుతుంది. Cut వద్ద సరికొత్త north మరియు south poles వస్తాయి.

tts_sentences.text_en: There is no magnetic monopole. Keep cutting and you always get two poles — never a lone north or south.
tts_sentences.text_hi: कोई magnetic monopole नहीं होता। काटते रहिए और आपको हमेशा two poles मिलते हैं — कभी अकेला north या south नहीं।
tts_sentences.text_te: Magnetic monopole అనేది లేదు. కట్ చేస్తూ ఉంటే మీకు ఎల్లప్పుడూ two poles వస్తాయి — ఎప్పుడూ ఒంటరి north లేదా south కాదు.

misconception_watch.belief: If you cut a bar magnet in half you can isolate a single north pole and a single south pole.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: The magnet splits at the centre and new N and S poles light up at the cut faces, so each half is a complete dipole with its own field — repeated to show it always happens.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: There is no magnetic monopole: every cut produces two complete dipoles, each with a north AND a south pole.

STATE_4.title: The magnetic moment m — pointing S to N

scene_composition.text: Give the magnet one arrow that captures its strength + direction

A single vector describes the
whole dipole. Which way
should it point — from N to S,
or from S to N?

The magnetic moment m points
from S to N, along the axis.
Its size measures how strong
the magnet is.
Magnetic moment m: points S → N, magnitude m = N·I·A (the equivalent solenoid)

tts_sentences.text_en: We capture the whole dipole with a single vector — the magnetic moment, written m.
tts_sentences.text_hi: हम पूरे dipole को एक single vector से दर्शाते हैं — magnetic moment, जिसे m लिखते हैं।
tts_sentences.text_te: మనం మొత్తం dipole ను ఒక single vector తో సూచిస్తాం — magnetic moment, దీన్ని m అని రాస్తాం.

tts_sentences.text_en: Which way should the moment m point — from north to south, or from south to north?
tts_sentences.text_hi: Moment m को किस ओर point करना चाहिए — north से south, या south से north?
tts_sentences.text_te: Moment m ఏ వైపు point చేయాలి — north నుండి south, లేదా south నుండి north?

tts_sentences.text_en: The magnetic moment m points from the south pole to the north pole, along the magnet's axis.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Magnetic moment m, south pole से north pole की ओर, magnet के axis के साथ point करता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: Magnetic moment m, south pole నుండి north pole వైపు, magnet axis వెంట point చేస్తుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: Its magnitude is m equals N times I times A — the same moment as the equivalent solenoid.
tts_sentences.text_hi: इसका magnitude है m equals N times I times A — equivalent solenoid जैसा ही moment।
tts_sentences.text_te: దాని magnitude m equals N times I times A — equivalent solenoid వంటి moment.

misconception_watch.belief: The magnetic moment points from the north pole to the south pole, like a field line outside the magnet.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: The moment arrow m is drawn along the axis pointing from the S pole to the N pole, distinct from the external field-line direction.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: The magnetic moment m points from S to N (inside-to-outside sense), with magnitude m = NIA — the same as the equivalent solenoid.

STATE_5.title: A bar magnet ≡ a solenoid (m = NIA)

scene_composition.text: Slip a current-carrying solenoid over the magnet's place

A solenoid of N turns carrying
current I also has an N end
and an S end. How does its
far field compare?

Identical. A bar magnet is
equivalent to a solenoid of
moment m = N·I·A — the same
far field, the same dipole.
Bar magnet ≡ solenoid: the magnetism of matter comes from tiny current loops (m = N·I·A)

tts_sentences.text_en: Slip a solenoid — a coil of N turns carrying current I — into the magnet's place.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Magnet की जगह एक solenoid रखिए — N turns की coil जिसमें current I बहता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: Magnet స్థానంలో ఒక solenoid ను ఉంచండి — current I ప్రవహించే N turns coil.

tts_sentences.text_en: The solenoid also has a north end and a south end. How does its far field compare to the magnet's?
tts_sentences.text_hi: Solenoid के भी north और south end होते हैं। इसका far field magnet से कैसे compare होता है?
tts_sentences.text_te: Solenoid కి కూడా north మరియు south end ఉంటాయి. దాని far field magnet తో ఎలా compare అవుతుంది?

tts_sentences.text_en: Identical. A bar magnet is equivalent to a solenoid of magnetic moment m equals N times I times A.
tts_sentences.text_hi: बिल्कुल समान। एक bar magnet, magnetic moment m equals N times I times A वाले solenoid के equivalent है।
tts_sentences.text_te: సరిగ్గా సమానం. ఒక bar magnet, magnetic moment m equals N times I times A ఉన్న solenoid కి equivalent.

tts_sentences.text_en: The magnetism of matter itself comes from countless tiny atomic current loops — every magnet is, at heart, current.
tts_sentences.text_hi: पदार्थ का magnetism भी अनगिनत tiny atomic current loops से आता है — हर magnet मूल रूप से current है।
tts_sentences.text_te: పదార్థం యొక్క magnetism కూడా లెక్కలేనన్ని tiny atomic current loops నుండి వస్తుంది — ప్రతి magnet మూలంలో current.

misconception_watch.belief: A permanent magnet's magnetism is something entirely different from electric current.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: A solenoid of current loops fades onto the magnet's axis and produces the same dipole field, with the moment labelled m = NIA.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: A bar magnet is equivalent to a current-carrying solenoid of moment m = NIA; magnetism in matter comes from tiny atomic current loops.

scene_composition.text: Measure the field at the same distance r — on the axis vs to the side

Two probes at equal distance r:
one on the axis (axial), one
perpendicular (equatorial).
Which field is stronger?

The axial field is exactly
TWICE the equatorial field.
Both fade as 1/r³ — a dipole,
not a 1/r² point charge.
B_axial = (μ₀/4π)(2m/r³)   B_equatorial = (μ₀/4π)(m/r³)   →   axial : equatorial = 2 : 1

tts_sentences.text_en: Place two probes at the same distance r: one on the axis, one perpendicular, on the equatorial line.
tts_sentences.text_hi: एक ही distance r पर दो probes रखिए: एक axis पर, एक perpendicular, equatorial line पर।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఒకే distance r వద్ద రెండు probes ఉంచండి: ఒకటి axis పై, ఒకటి perpendicular, equatorial line పై.

tts_sentences.text_en: Both are equally far from the magnet. Predict: which field is stronger — the axial or the equatorial?
tts_sentences.text_hi: दोनों magnet से समान दूरी पर हैं। Predict कीजिए: कौन सा field stronger है — axial या equatorial?
tts_sentences.text_te: రెండూ magnet నుండి సమాన దూరంలో ఉన్నాయి. Predict చేయండి: ఏ field stronger — axial లేదా equatorial?

tts_sentences.text_en: The axial field is exactly twice the equatorial field, and both fade as one over r cubed.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Axial field, equatorial field से ठीक दोगुना है, और दोनों one over r cubed के अनुसार fade होते हैं।
tts_sentences.text_te: Axial field, equatorial field కంటే సరిగ్గా రెట్టింపు, మరియు రెండూ one over r cubed ప్రకారం fade అవుతాయి.

tts_sentences.text_en: That one over r cubed falloff is the signature of a dipole — far steeper than a point charge's one over r squared.
tts_sentences.text_hi: वह one over r cubed falloff एक dipole की पहचान है — point charge के one over r squared से कहीं तेज़।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఆ one over r cubed falloff ఒక dipole యొక్క గుర్తు — point charge యొక్క one over r squared కంటే చాలా ఎక్కువ.

misconception_watch.belief: The magnetic field is the same in all directions at a given distance, or falls as 1/r² like a point charge.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: At equal r the axial probe arrow is twice as long as the equatorial one, and as r sweeps outward both arrows shrink as 1/r³.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: A dipole field is direction-dependent: B_axial = 2·B_equatorial at the same r, and both fall as 1/r³, steeper than a point charge's 1/r².

STATE_7.title: The electrostatic analog — a magnetic version of the electric dipole

scene_composition.text: Compare the bar magnet with a +q / −q electric dipole

An electric dipole has the
same 1/r³ field, axial twice
equatorial. What plays the
role of the moment?

The same pattern exactly.
The magnetic moment m plays
the role of the electric
dipole moment p.
Electrostatic analog:  m ↔ p,  μ₀/4π ↔ 1/4πε₀  — identical dipole field

tts_sentences.text_en: Beside the magnet, picture a small electric dipole — a plus charge and a minus charge.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Magnet के बगल में, एक छोटा electric dipole सोचिए — एक plus charge और एक minus charge।
tts_sentences.text_te: Magnet పక్కన, ఒక చిన్న electric dipole ను ఊహించండి — ఒక plus charge మరియు ఒక minus charge.

tts_sentences.text_en: It has the same one over r cubed field, with axial twice equatorial. What plays the role of the moment?
tts_sentences.text_hi: इसका भी same one over r cubed field है, axial equatorial से दोगुना। Moment की भूमिका कौन निभाता है?
tts_sentences.text_te: దానికి కూడా same one over r cubed field, axial equatorial కంటే రెట్టింపు. Moment పాత్రను ఎవరు పోషిస్తారు?

tts_sentences.text_en: The pattern is identical. The magnetic moment m plays exactly the role of the electric dipole moment p.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Pattern बिल्कुल समान है। Magnetic moment m, electric dipole moment p की भूमिका निभाता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: Pattern సరిగ్గా సమానం. Magnetic moment m, electric dipole moment p పాత్రను పోషిస్తుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: So a bar magnet is the magnetic analog of an electric dipole, with mu zero over four pi replacing one over four pi epsilon zero.
tts_sentences.text_hi: तो bar magnet, electric dipole का magnetic analog है, जहाँ mu zero over four pi, one over four pi epsilon zero की जगह लेता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: కాబట్టి bar magnet, electric dipole యొక్క magnetic analog, ఇక్కడ mu zero over four pi, one over four pi epsilon zero స్థానంలో ఉంటుంది.

misconception_watch.belief: Magnetism and electrostatics are unrelated, with completely different field shapes.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: An electric dipole drawn beside the magnet shows the identical closed dipole field pattern, with the analogy m ↔ p, μ₀/4π ↔ 1/4πε₀ stated.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: A bar magnet is the exact magnetic analog of an electric dipole: m plays the role of p and μ₀/4π replaces 1/4πε₀, giving the same 1/r³ field.

scene_composition.text: Explore: drag the moment m and the distance r

Watch the axial and equatorial
probe arrows. The axial one
stays twice the equatorial,
at every distance.

Challenge:
Double the distance r — by
what factor does the field
drop? (1/r³ → ÷8)
B_axial ∝ 2m/r³ · B_equatorial ∝ m/r³ · ratio always 2 : 1

tts_sentences.text_en: Your turn. Drag the magnetic moment m and the distance r, and watch the two probe arrows.
tts_sentences.text_hi: अब आपकी बारी। Magnetic moment m और distance r को drag कीजिए, और दोनों probe arrows देखिए।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఇప్పుడు మీ వంతు. Magnetic moment m మరియు distance r ను drag చేయండి, రెండు probe arrows చూడండి.

tts_sentences.text_en: The axial arrow stays twice the equatorial one at every distance, and both shrink fast as r grows.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Axial arrow हर distance पर equatorial से दोगुना रहता है, और दोनों r बढ़ने पर तेज़ी से shrink होते हैं।
tts_sentences.text_te: Axial arrow ప్రతి distance వద్ద equatorial కంటే రెట్టింపు ఉంటుంది, మరియు r పెరిగే కొద్దీ రెండూ వేగంగా shrink అవుతాయి.

tts_sentences.text_en: Double the distance and the field drops to one eighth, because the dipole field falls as one over r cubed.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Distance दोगुना कीजिए और field one eighth रह जाता है, क्योंकि dipole field one over r cubed के अनुसार गिरता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: Distance రెట్టింపు చేస్తే field one eighth కి పడిపోతుంది, ఎందుకంటే dipole field one over r cubed ప్రకారం తగ్గుతుంది.

STATE_1.label: Bar magnet + closed-loop field, tracers drifting, compass settling
STATE_1.caption: A bar magnet (N red, S blue). Field B in green; amber dots drift out of N into S; a compass settles tangent.

STATE_2.label: One field line traced as a CLOSED loop (external arc + internal return)
STATE_2.caption: Follow one line: out of N, around to S, then through the magnet from S back to N — a continuous closed loop.

STATE_3.label: Break the magnet → two complete dipoles (new poles at the cut), looping
STATE_3.caption: Cut it in half and each piece is a complete magnet — new N and S appear at the cut. No isolated pole exists.

STATE_4.label: The magnetic moment m arrow (S → N) + field
STATE_4.caption: The magnetic moment m points from S to N along the axis; its magnitude m = NIA captures the magnet's strength.

STATE_5.label: Equivalent solenoid morph (m = NIA) over the magnet
STATE_5.caption: A solenoid of N turns carrying current I has the same far field: a bar magnet ≡ a solenoid of moment m = NIA.

STATE_6.label: Axial vs equatorial probes: 2:1 at equal r, r-sweep shows 1/r³
STATE_6.caption: At equal distance r, the axial field is twice the equatorial field; as r sweeps out, both shrink as 1/r³.

STATE_7.label: Electric-dipole analog inset beside the magnet (m ↔ p)
STATE_7.caption: An electric dipole shows the identical 1/r³ pattern: m plays the role of p, and μ₀/4π replaces 1/4πε₀.

STATE_8.label: Sandbox — m, r sliders with a live axial/equatorial B readout
STATE_8.caption: Drag m and r. Axial ∝ 2m/r³ and equatorial ∝ m/r³ update live — the ratio stays 2 : 1, both fall as 1/r³.

regeneration_variants.label: The compass needle
regeneration_variants.physical_world: The small magnetic compass needle from your geography or physics class
regeneration_variants.same_physics: The needle is a tiny bar magnet — a magnetic dipole with its own moment m — whose field is the same closed-loop dipole field, and which lines up tangent to any external field
regeneration_variants.scene_hint: A pivoted compass needle, red N end, with the same closed dipole field lines around it, settling along an external field.

regeneration_variants.label: Snapping a fridge magnet
regeneration_variants.physical_world: Breaking a fridge magnet or a flexible magnetic strip in half
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Each broken piece is instantly a complete dipole with a new N and S at the break — the no-monopole rule, identical to cutting a bar magnet
regeneration_variants.scene_hint: A magnetic strip snapping, each half lighting up new N and S poles at the fracture with its own field loops.

assessment.mastery_definition: A student has mastered this concept when they can explain that a bar magnet is a magnetic DIPOLE whose field lines are continuous CLOSED loops (out of N, around outside to S, and through the magnet from S back to N), so a line never starts or ends — unlike electric field lines, which begin on positive charge and end on negative; that there is NO magnetic monopole, so cutting a magnet always yields two complete dipoles with new poles appearing at the cut; that the magnetic moment m points from S to N with magnitude m = NIA, the bar magnet being EQUIVALENT to a solenoid of that moment (magnetism in matter arising from tiny atomic current loops); that the dipole far field falls as 1/r³ with the axial field exactly TWICE the equatorial field at the same distance (B_axial = (μ₀/4π)(2m/r³), B_equatorial = (μ₀/4π)(m/r³)); and that this is the exact electrostatic analog of an electric dipole, with m ↔ p and μ₀/4π ↔ 1/4πε₀.

questions.stem: How do a bar magnet's field lines differ from an electric dipole's field lines?

options.A: Magnetic field lines are continuous closed loops (no start or end); electric field lines start on + charge and end on − charge
options.B: Magnetic field lines start at N and end at S, exactly like electric field lines on charges
options.C: Magnetic field lines are straight; electric field lines are curved
options.D: There is no difference — both are closed loops

distractor_misconceptions.B: Treats N and S as field-line sources/sinks like + and − charges, missing that magnetic lines close through the magnet
distractor_misconceptions.C: Confuses field-line shape with the real distinction (open vs closed)
distractor_misconceptions.D: Misses that electric field lines are open (start/end on charge), not closed

questions.tested_idea: Magnetic field lines are continuous closed loops; electric field lines start and end on charges.
questions.parallel_form_stem: Trace a magnetic field line of a bar magnet from the north pole. Describe where it goes and whether it ever ends, and contrast with an electric field line.

questions.stem: You cut a bar magnet exactly in half at its middle. What do you get?

options.A: Two complete magnets, each with its own N and S — new poles appear at the cut
options.B: One isolated north pole and one isolated south pole
options.C: Two pieces that are each unmagnetised
options.D: One magnet and one non-magnetic piece

distractor_misconceptions.B: Believes a single pole (a monopole) can be isolated by cutting
distractor_misconceptions.C: Thinks cutting destroys the magnetism rather than creating two dipoles
distractor_misconceptions.D: Thinks only one half keeps the magnetism

questions.tested_idea: No magnetic monopole exists: cutting a magnet yields two complete dipoles.
questions.parallel_form_stem: A long bar magnet is broken into three pieces. How many poles does each piece have, and why can you never obtain a single isolated pole?

questions.stem: In which direction does the magnetic moment m of a bar magnet point, and what sets its magnitude?

options.A: From S to N along the axis, with magnitude m = NIA (the equivalent solenoid)
options.B: From N to S along the axis, like a field line outside the magnet
options.C: Perpendicular to the axis, in the direction of the equatorial field
options.D: It has no definite direction; only its magnitude matters

distractor_misconceptions.B: Reverses the moment to N→S, confusing it with the external field-line direction
distractor_misconceptions.C: Confuses the moment's axis with the equatorial field direction
distractor_misconceptions.D: Treats the moment as a scalar, missing that it is a vector S→N

questions.tested_idea: The magnetic moment m points S→N with magnitude m = NIA.
questions.parallel_form_stem: Define the magnetic moment of a bar magnet: state its direction relative to the poles and the formula that gives its magnitude via the equivalent solenoid.

questions.stem: A bar magnet is said to be equivalent to what, and why does that matter?

options.A: A solenoid of moment m = NIA producing the same far field — magnetism in matter comes from tiny current loops
options.B: A single point charge, since both have a 1/r² field
options.C: A pair of isolated monopoles held a fixed distance apart
options.D: A straight current-carrying wire, since both involve current

distractor_misconceptions.B: Confuses a dipole (1/r³) with a point charge (1/r²)
distractor_misconceptions.C: Reintroduces monopoles, which do not exist
distractor_misconceptions.D: Picks a wire (1/r field, not a dipole) instead of a solenoid

questions.tested_idea: A bar magnet is equivalent to a solenoid of moment m = NIA; magnetism arises from current loops.
questions.parallel_form_stem: Explain in what sense a bar magnet and a current-carrying solenoid are equivalent, and what this says about the origin of magnetism in matter.

questions.stem: At the same distance r from a bar magnet, how does the axial field compare to the equatorial field, and how do they depend on r?

options.B: They are equal, and both fall as 1/r²

distractor_misconceptions.B: Assumes the field is isotropic and uses a point-charge 1/r² falloff
distractor_misconceptions.C: Inverts the ratio, making equatorial the stronger one
distractor_misconceptions.D: Gets the 2:1 ratio but uses the wrong (point-charge) 1/r² falloff

questions.tested_idea: B_axial = 2·B_equatorial at equal r, and both fall as 1/r³ (a dipole field).
questions.parallel_form_stem: Two points are equally distant from a short bar magnet, one on its axis and one on its perpendicular bisector. Compare the magnetic field magnitudes and state how each scales with distance.

questions.stem: What is the electrostatic analog of a bar magnet, and how do the quantities correspond?

options.A: An electric dipole: the magnetic moment m plays the role of p, and μ₀/4π replaces 1/4πε₀
options.B: A single point charge, with m playing the role of the charge q
options.C: A uniformly charged sphere, with m playing the role of the total charge
options.D: There is no analog — magnetism is unrelated to electrostatics

distractor_misconceptions.B: Maps the magnet to a monopole/point charge instead of a dipole
distractor_misconceptions.C: Chooses a charge distribution with a different (non-dipole) field

questions.tested_idea: A bar magnet is the magnetic analog of an electric dipole (m ↔ p, μ₀/4π ↔ 1/4πε₀).
questions.parallel_form_stem: State the electrostatic analog of a bar magnet and give the correspondence between the magnetic and electric quantities in the far-field dipole formulas.
